title: User-centred design
objectives: Define user-centred design (UCD) and explain the philosophy underlying it.
Describe the iterative nature of the UCD process.
Explain the difference between user needs, user wants, and design requirements.
Analyse how placing users at the centre of the design process improves design outcomes.
Evaluate the limitations of user-centred design as a design philosophy.
summary:
User-centred design (UCD) is a design philosophy and process in which the needs, preferences, limitations, and context of end users are given primary consideration at every stage of the design process. The principle is that design decisions should be grounded in an empirical understanding of real users, rather than in designers' assumptions about what users want or need. The concept was articulated and systematised by Donald Norman and his colleagues, particularly in Norman's 1988 book 'The Design of Everyday Things', which argued that the failure of designed products is almost always a design failure — a failure to understand users — rather than a user failure.

UCD is fundamentally iterative. Rather than proceeding linearly from brief to finished product, UCD cycles through stages of user research, design, prototyping, and testing repeatedly. Each cycle generates new understanding of user needs and reveals problems with the current design, informing the next iteration. This iterative approach acknowledges that even well-intentioned designers cannot fully understand user behaviour and context without empirical observation and testing. The number of iteration cycles depends on the complexity of the product and the resources available, but the principle is to make cycles as short and inexpensive as possible, especially in early stages.

A critical distinction in UCD is between user needs, user wants, and design requirements. User needs are the genuine underlying requirements that a product must fulfil — often unarticulated and requiring research to uncover. User wants are what users say they want — conscious desires that may or may not reflect underlying needs. Henry Ford's apocryphal quote ('if I had asked people what they wanted, they would have said faster horses') illustrates the risk of taking user wants literally. Design requirements are the specific, measurable criteria that a design must meet, derived from user needs, technical constraints, business requirements, and regulatory standards. Translating user needs into design requirements is a key skill in UCD practice.

UCD improves design outcomes by reducing the gap between designed functionality and actual user behaviour — people use products in ways designers do not anticipate, in environments designers have not considered, with levels of skill that differ from what was assumed. By grounding design decisions in empirical user research and iterative testing, UCD reduces this gap and reduces the risk of expensive redesign after launch or product failure due to poor usability. Evidence from many industries (healthcare, software, consumer electronics) shows that investment in UCD reduces support costs, improves user satisfaction, and increases product adoption rates.

Limitations of UCD include: it can be time-consuming and expensive, particularly if many iteration cycles are required; existing users may not articulate needs for truly radical innovations (users cannot imagine what they have not experienced); UCD may not adequately represent the interests of non-users affected by the design (e.g. workers, environment); it can lead to incremental rather than radical innovation if constrained by existing user mental models; and in some contexts, designing for the 'typical' user may still exclude minority user groups despite the UCD label.

quiz.question: Define user-centred design and explain why Donald Norman argued that product failure is almost always a design failure rather than a user failure.
quiz.markscheme: UCD is a design philosophy placing user needs, context, and behaviour at the centre of every design decision, grounded in empirical user research. Norman argued that when people cannot use a product, the problem lies in the design — poor affordances, lack of feedback, or mismatch with users' mental models — not in the users' intelligence or ability. Blaming users ('user error') ignores the designer's responsibility to create products that are self-explanatory and match real human capabilities and behaviour. This argument shifted the frame from 'how do we train users to use this?' to 'how do we design products that users can use without training?'

quiz.question: Explain why the UCD process is described as iterative and describe what happens in each cycle.
quiz.markscheme: Iterative because understanding of user needs and design problems deepens progressively — no single design cycle can anticipate all user behaviour and requirements. Each iteration cycle: (1) conduct/refine user research to understand needs and context; (2) generate or refine design concepts; (3) build a prototype at appropriate fidelity; (4) test prototype with representative users; (5) analyse findings, identify problems and insights; return to step 1 or 2. Cycles are made as short and cheap as possible in early stages (low-fidelity prototypes, small user samples) and progressively higher fidelity as the design converges. The number of cycles depends on complexity and resources.

quiz.question: Distinguish between user needs, user wants, and design requirements, using an example from product design.
quiz.markscheme: User needs: genuine underlying requirements — often unarticulated. E.g. for a commuter, the need is to arrive at work on time and unstressed. User wants: what users consciously ask for — e.g. 'I want the bus to come every 5 minutes.' Design requirements: specific, measurable criteria derived from needs, technical constraints, business requirements, and regulations — e.g. 'The mobile app must display the next three departures within 2 seconds of opening.' A key skill in UCD is translating unarticulated needs into testable requirements — taking user wants as data points, not literal specifications.

quiz.question: Analyse how a UCD approach would improve the design of a medical device used by elderly patients at home.
quiz.markscheme: A UCD approach would: conduct ethnographic research in patients' homes to understand their actual context of use, physical capabilities, visual acuity, and cognitive patterns; identify unarticulated needs (e.g. difficulty reading small text, fear of making errors with medication dosing); involve patients in iterative prototype testing; design for reduced physical and cognitive demands (large buttons, clear visual feedback, error-tolerant design); test with representative elderly users under realistic conditions. Without UCD, a device designed by engineers to technical specifications may be unusable by elderly patients — leading to medication errors, hospitalisation, and product abandonment. UCD evidence shows significantly reduced error rates and improved adherence when devices are designed with users.

quiz.question: Evaluate two limitations of user-centred design as a design approach.
quiz.markscheme: Accept any two with evaluation: Cost and time — iterative user research and testing cycles require significant resource investment; this may be impractical for small organisations or short development timescales. Limitation on radical innovation — users are constrained by existing experience and cannot articulate needs for truly novel technologies they have not encountered; UCD risks optimising around existing solutions rather than enabling breakthroughs. Representativeness — UCD user samples may not fully represent the diversity of the user population, risking design that works for the majority but excludes minorities or marginalised groups. Narrow stakeholder focus — optimising for the user may underweight impacts on non-users (workers, communities, environment). Each limitation should be explained and an implication given.

title: Strategies for user research
objectives: Describe the main qualitative and quantitative user research methods.
Explain when ethnographic observation is more appropriate than surveys or interviews.
Describe how personas and user journey maps are created and used.
Analyse the limitations of different user research methods.
Evaluate the importance of recruiting representative research participants.
summary:
User research is the systematic process of gathering information about users, their behaviour, needs, contexts, and motivations to inform design decisions. It spans a range of methods from large-scale quantitative surveys to intimate ethnographic observation, and the choice of method depends on the questions being asked, the stage of design, the resources available, and the nature of the user population.

Quantitative research methods generate numerical data that can be statistically analysed. Surveys (questionnaires distributed to large samples) measure stated preferences, attitudes, and usage patterns across a broad population. Analytics (tracking how users actually interact with a digital product) reveal behavioural patterns at scale. A/B testing compares two versions of a design feature with live users to determine which performs better. These methods can achieve large sample sizes and statistical significance, but they measure what users report or do in standardised conditions — not why they behave as they do.

Qualitative research methods generate rich, contextual understanding of user behaviour, motivations, and needs. Structured, semi-structured, and unstructured interviews allow users to describe their experiences and needs in depth. Contextual inquiry (observing and interviewing users in their actual environment while they perform tasks) reveals behaviours and contextual factors that users would not think to mention in a laboratory setting. Usability testing (observing users attempt to perform specific tasks with a prototype or existing product) directly measures usability and reveals specific interaction problems. Focus groups bring together a small number of users to discuss a topic or react to a design — useful for exploring attitudes but prone to social dynamics that suppress minority views.

Ethnographic observation — watching users in their natural environment without intervention — is the most powerful method for revealing unarticulated needs and the gap between what users say they do and what they actually do. It is time-consuming and resource-intensive, making it most appropriate early in the design process for ill-defined problems, or when existing products are known to have usability problems whose cause is not understood from quantitative data alone.

Personas are composite fictional user profiles created from user research data to represent key user groups. A persona includes demographic information, goals, frustrations, behaviours, and context. They are used as a communication tool — keeping the team aligned on who is being designed for — and as a design evaluation tool ('would Maya, our primary persona, find this step confusing?'). User journey maps visualise the sequence of steps, interactions, emotions, and pain points a user experiences through a complete service or product interaction — from first awareness through purchase, use, and disposal. They reveal gaps, inconsistencies, and opportunities across the whole user experience, not just the product in isolation.

Representative sampling is critical to reliable user research. Research conducted only with easily accessible users (e.g. design students, company employees, social media-recruited volunteers) may systematically misrepresent the actual user population in terms of age, technical literacy, physical capability, cultural background, or usage context. Poor sampling produces misleading data that directs design away from real user needs.

quiz.question: Explain when ethnographic observation would be more appropriate than a survey for understanding user needs.
quiz.markscheme: Ethnographic observation is more appropriate when: the research question concerns actual behaviour (not self-reported behaviour, which is often inaccurate); when users are unlikely to be able to articulate their needs or problems verbally; when the context of use significantly affects the interaction (e.g. a factory floor, a hospital ward, a kitchen); when the problem is ill-defined and the research goal is to discover what the real questions are; and when understanding the gap between what users say they do and what they actually do is important. Surveys are more appropriate when the goal is to measure attitudes or preferences at scale, when statistical analysis across a large sample is needed, or when the research question is already well-defined.

quiz.question: Describe what a persona is, explain how it is created from user research data, and identify one advantage of using personas in a design team.
quiz.markscheme: A persona is a composite, fictional user profile representing a key user group, created by synthesising patterns from user research (interviews, observations, surveys). It includes: name, demographic background, goals, frustrations/pain points, behaviours, technical literacy, and context of use. Creation process: conduct user research, identify patterns and distinct user types across participants, create one persona per major user type, validate against research data. Advantage: serves as a shared communication tool within the design team, keeping everyone aligned on who is being designed for and reducing the risk of designing for a hypothetical 'average user' or the designers themselves.

quiz.question: Describe how a user journey map is constructed and explain how it can reveal design opportunities.
quiz.markscheme: A user journey map is constructed by: defining the scenario (a specific user completing a specific task or experience); mapping the stages of the journey chronologically; for each stage, identifying: actions the user takes, touchpoints (where they interact with the product/service), thoughts, emotions, and pain points (moments of frustration or confusion). Data comes from user research. The map reveals: where the emotional experience drops (pain points that need to be addressed); inconsistencies across channels; steps where users are confused or drop off; and 'moments of delight' that should be preserved or amplified. Design opportunities cluster around pain points and gaps — these are the highest-priority areas for design intervention.

quiz.question: Analyse two limitations of using focus groups as a user research method in product design.
quiz.markscheme: Social dynamics: dominant personalities suppress minority views; group consensus forms around the opinions of the most vocal participants — individual perspectives are lost. Participants may not want to disagree publicly or admit to being confused by a product. Social desirability bias: participants give answers they think the moderator wants to hear, or that make them appear competent (particularly relevant for admitting difficulties with technology). Additionally, focus groups measure verbal responses to discussed design concepts — not actual interaction behaviour. These are significant limitations because most design decisions benefit from observational (not verbal) data about real user behaviour.

quiz.question: Evaluate the importance of recruiting representative research participants in user research.
quiz.markscheme: Unrepresentative samples systematically misrepresent actual user populations — research conducted with young, technically literate, highly educated participants (e.g. design students, company employees) underrepresents older users, people with lower literacy, physical or cognitive disabilities, or users from different cultural backgrounds. Design decisions based on unrepresentative samples optimise for the sampled group and may create products that are unusable or inappropriate for the broader user population. This is particularly consequential for critical applications (medical devices, safety equipment) and when the intended user population is significantly different from typical 'convenient' research participants. Representative sampling is therefore fundamental to the validity of user research findings and the quality of resulting designs.

title: Strategies for user-centred design
objectives: Describe participatory design and explain how it differs from user research.
Explain how the design brief is developed from user research findings.
Describe the role of usability testing in the UCD process.
Analyse how iterative prototyping enables continuous design improvement.
Evaluate the application of UCD principles to a specific complex design challenge.
summary:
User-centred design is not merely about conducting user research before designing — it requires user involvement throughout the design process, including in ideation, prototype evaluation, and refinement. Several distinct strategies characterise advanced UCD practice, from participatory co-design to rigorous usability evaluation frameworks.

Participatory design (also called co-design) involves users as active co-designers rather than merely as research subjects. Users participate in ideation workshops, collaborative design exercises, and critique sessions. This approach originated in Scandinavian workplace democracy movements of the 1970s, where workers were given a voice in the design of the tools and systems they would use. In modern product and service design, participatory approaches range from community engagement in public space design to co-creation workshops with target consumers. Participatory design is particularly valuable for: complex sociotechnical systems where users have deep domain knowledge; marginalised communities whose needs are unlikely to be understood by designers from different backgrounds; and situations where user acceptance and adoption are critical to success.

The design brief — the document defining what the design should achieve — is most powerful when it is derived from user research findings rather than from client assumptions. A research-informed brief identifies the specific user groups, their needs and contexts, the problems to be solved, and the constraints (technical, budget, regulatory, time). It is typically framed as a set of design requirements — specific, testable criteria against which design solutions can be evaluated — rather than as prescriptive solutions. Writing 'the product must allow a seated user to adjust the height range from 60 cm to 110 cm with one hand' is a better requirement than 'the product should have a height-adjustment mechanism.'

Usability testing is the systematic process of observing representative users attempting to complete defined tasks with a prototype or product, to identify usability problems. A usability test specifies: the user profile (who is being tested), the tasks (what they will be asked to do), the evaluation criteria (success/failure rates, time on task, error rates, satisfaction scores), and the analysis approach. Usability testing can be conducted in laboratory conditions (controlled, observable) or 'in the wild' (in users' natural environment, more realistic but harder to observe closely). Think-aloud protocols (asking users to verbalise their thoughts while using the product) provide rich qualitative data on the mental models and confusion points that are not visible from behaviour alone.

Iterative prototyping means building a prototype, testing it with users, learning from the findings, and building an improved version — repeating as many times as resources allow. The fidelity of prototypes increases over iteration cycles: early iterations use low-fidelity paper prototypes or foam models; later iterations use high-fidelity interactive digital or physical prototypes. The value of each iteration is the learning it generates: even a single usability test with five users reliably reveals approximately 85% of usability problems with an interface, according to Nielsen's research. The key is acting on findings promptly — prototyping value is only realised when problems discovered are fixed in the next iteration.

The IDEO design process, the British Design Council's Double Diamond, and ISO 9241-210 (Ergonomics of Human-System Interaction — Human-centred design for interactive systems) all codify versions of UCD principles. ISO 9241-210 is the international standard for human-centred design process, defining six key principles: the design is based on explicit understanding of users, tasks, and environments; users are involved throughout design and development; the design is driven and refined by user-centred evaluation; the process is iterative; the design addresses the whole user experience; and the design team includes multidisciplinary skills.

quiz.question: Distinguish between participatory design and conventional user research, explaining why participatory design is particularly valuable in some contexts.
quiz.markscheme: Conventional user research treats users as research subjects — designers observe and interview users to understand needs, then design independently. Participatory design treats users as co-designers — they actively participate in ideation and design development. Particularly valuable when: users have deep domain knowledge that designers lack (e.g. workers designing factory equipment); the user community is marginalised and unlikely to be well-understood by designers from different backgrounds; or user acceptance is critical to success (participation builds ownership and commitment). Limitation: managing diverse participant inputs is complex, and participants' design suggestions may not be technically feasible.

quiz.question: Explain how a design brief derived from user research differs from a conventional client-generated brief.
quiz.markscheme: A conventional client-generated brief typically specifies what the client wants (often a product description or feature list), reflecting the client's assumptions about user needs. A user research-derived brief specifies: who the users are (with supporting data); what their genuine needs and contexts are (from research); the problems to be solved (framed from the user's perspective); and specific, testable design requirements (measurable criteria for success). This shifts the frame from 'what does the client want?' to 'what do users need?' and provides a more rigorous basis for evaluating design options. The research-informed brief may challenge client assumptions — e.g. research may reveal that users need simpler operation, not more features.

quiz.question: Describe the think-aloud protocol in usability testing and explain what data it generates.
quiz.markscheme: In a think-aloud protocol, users are asked to verbalise their thoughts continuously while using a prototype or product — saying what they are looking at, what they are thinking, what they are trying to do, and why they make each action. This externalises users' mental models, reveals where they are confused or uncertain, surfaces incorrect assumptions about how the system works, and identifies language or labelling mismatches. The data is qualitative — verbatim transcripts and observations — which are analysed to identify recurring themes and interaction problems. Think-aloud data complements quantitative usability metrics (success rate, time on task) by explaining why problems occur, not just that they occur.

quiz.question: Analyse why testing a prototype with even a small number of users (e.g. five) can be highly valuable in UCD practice.
quiz.markscheme: Jakob Nielsen's research found that five users in a usability test reliably reveal approximately 85% of usability problems with an interface — each additional user beyond five adds diminishing returns. This is because usability problems tend to be systematic (most users experience the same confusions) and the goal of early usability testing is to identify major problems, not achieve statistical precision. Testing with five users iteratively — fixing problems between tests — is more effective than one large test. Small tests are also faster and cheaper, supporting rapid iteration. The key implication is that UCD does not require large, expensive studies to generate actionable findings at the design iteration stage — early and frequent small tests outperform rare large tests.

quiz.question: Evaluate the application of UCD principles to the design of an automated self-checkout system for a supermarket.
quiz.markscheme: Application of UCD: user research would reveal that users have varying levels of technology confidence; elderly users, users with physical limitations, and users with screened vision may face specific challenges; users are often in a time-pressured, noisy, unfamiliar environment; they bring strong mental models from human-served checkout. UCD would identify these contexts and design to support them: clear, legible instructions; error tolerance (undoing accidental actions); clear feedback at each step; physical design accommodating wheelchairs and different heights. However, limitations: self-checkout fundamentally changes the service model in ways users may not prefer — UCD cannot always overcome structural service design constraints; and some user groups (elderly, disabled) may find any automated checkout less accessible regardless of design iteration. A full evaluation would weigh UCD's contributions against systemic constraints.

title: Sustainable development
objectives: Define sustainable development with reference to the Brundtland Commission definition.
Explain the three pillars of sustainability: environmental, social, and economic.
Describe the role of designers in contributing to sustainable development.
Analyse the tensions between economic growth and environmental sustainability.
Discuss the concept of intergenerational equity in sustainable development.
summary:
Sustainable development is defined by the Brundtland Commission (World Commission on Environment and Development, 1987) as 'development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.' This definition introduces two key concepts: the idea of 'needs' (particularly the essential needs of the world's poor) and the idea of 'limitations' imposed by the state of technology and social organisation on the environment's ability to meet present and future needs. It is a fundamentally ethical concept, asserting the rights of future generations and the obligations of current decision-makers.

Sustainability is commonly conceptualised as having three pillars or dimensions, sometimes called the 'triple bottom line': environmental sustainability (maintaining the natural systems — climate, biodiversity, water, soil — that support all life and economic activity); social sustainability (ensuring that development is equitable, that the benefits and burdens are distributed fairly within and across generations, and that human well-being, rights, and cultural integrity are maintained); and economic sustainability (ensuring that economic activity can continue at a level that meets human needs without undermining the environmental and social foundations it depends on). These three dimensions are interdependent — environmental degradation undermines economic activity, economic inequality drives unsustainable resource exploitation, and social instability makes environmental governance impossible.

Designers are positioned at the point where decisions are made about what is made, from what materials, in what quantities, for how long, and how it is disposed of. These decisions collectively determine much of the environmental and social impact of the industrial economy. Research suggests that approximately 80% of a product's environmental impact is determined at the design stage — before a single unit is manufactured. Designers who understand sustainability can make choices that dramatically reduce resource and energy consumption, minimise waste, extend product life, and improve social outcomes for workers and users.

The tension between economic growth and environmental sustainability is one of the central challenges of the 21st century. Conventional economic growth typically requires increased resource throughput, energy consumption, and waste generation. Ecological economists argue that on a finite planet, infinite growth is physically impossible and that economic models must be redesigned around wellbeing and resource efficiency rather than GDP growth. The concepts of 'green growth' (decoupling economic growth from resource consumption through efficiency and technology) and 'degrowth' (deliberately reducing economic throughput in wealthy nations) represent competing responses to this tension.

Intergenerational equity is the principle that future generations have an equal right to access the world's natural and social resources. It is violated when current resource consumption depletes non-renewable resources, destabilises the climate, or degrades ecosystems in ways that limit future generations' options. Applying intergenerational equity to design decisions means asking: what will be the condition of this material in 50 years? Can it be recovered and reused? Does this product leave the world better or worse for future designers and users?

quiz.question: State the Brundtland Commission's definition of sustainable development and explain the two key concepts it introduces.
quiz.markscheme: 'Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.' Two key concepts: (1) 'needs' — emphasising that the priority is meeting human needs, particularly those of the world's poorest; (2) 'limitations' — acknowledging that technology and social organisation impose limits on the environment's capacity to meet present and future needs. The definition is notable for its ethical dimension — asserting obligations to future generations and to equitable distribution of development benefits.

quiz.question: Explain the three pillars of sustainability and describe how they are interdependent.
quiz.markscheme: Environmental: maintaining natural systems (climate stability, biodiversity, clean water, healthy soils) that support all life and economic activity. Social: equitable distribution of development benefits and burdens within and across generations; protection of human rights and cultural integrity. Economic: economic activity that meets human needs without depleting the environmental and social foundations it depends on. Interdependence: environmental degradation undermines economic productivity (soil erosion reduces agriculture; climate change disrupts supply chains); economic inequality drives unsustainable resource exploitation (poverty forces over-harvesting); social instability makes environmental governance impossible; ecological collapse causes economic and social collapse. All three pillars must be maintained simultaneously — improving one at the expense of another is not truly sustainable.

quiz.question: Analyse why designers have a particularly significant role in determining the sustainability of products.
quiz.markscheme: Research indicates that approximately 80% of a product's environmental impact is determined at the design stage — the choice of materials, product architecture, manufacturing process, energy requirements in use, and end-of-life treatment are all made by designers before production begins. This means that sustainability improvements made at the design stage have far greater leverage than improvements in manufacturing or logistics alone. Designers determine whether a product can be repaired, disassembled, or recycled; whether it uses renewable or scarce materials; whether it is energy-efficient in use; and how long it is designed to last. This gives designers both significant responsibility and significant opportunity for positive impact.

quiz.question: Discuss the tension between economic growth and environmental sustainability, with reference to the concept of decoupling.
quiz.markscheme: Conventional economic growth increases resource throughput, energy consumption, and waste generation — environmental impact tends to scale with GDP. Decoupling refers to separating economic growth from resource consumption or environmental impact: relative decoupling means resource use grows more slowly than GDP; absolute decoupling means resource use falls while GDP grows. Evidence for absolute decoupling at national scale is limited and contested — most apparent decoupling in wealthy nations partly reflects the offshoring of resource-intensive production. Ecological economists argue genuine sustainability requires going beyond efficiency improvements to redesigning economic systems. The tension is between the social benefits of growth (poverty reduction, investment capacity) and the physical limits of ecological systems. A 'discuss' answer should present both sides.

quiz.question: Explain the principle of intergenerational equity and describe how a product designer can apply it in practice.
quiz.markscheme: Intergenerational equity is the principle that future generations have equal rights to access natural and social resources — current decisions should not foreclose future options by depleting non-renewable resources, destabilising the climate, or degrading ecosystems irreversibly. In design practice: avoid specifying materials from critically declining non-renewable sources; design for durability and repairability to slow resource throughput; choose materials with viable recycling routes to preserve material value; minimise carbon emissions in manufacture and use (contributing to climate stability); design for disassembly so material value is preserved for future use. Applying intergenerational equity means asking 'what condition will we leave the world in?' for each design decision.

title: Sustainable design
objectives: Define sustainable design and describe its key principles.
Explain the concept of cradle-to-cradle design and how it differs from cradle-to-grave.
Describe the Design for Environment (DfE) approach and its key strategies.
Analyse how material selection decisions affect the sustainability of a product.
Evaluate the effectiveness of ecolabelling schemes in promoting sustainable design.
summary:
Sustainable design is the practice of designing products, services, and built environments in ways that reduce environmental impact, improve social outcomes, and remain economically viable — aligning design practice with the principles of sustainable development. It is not merely the application of a checklist of green features but requires a systems-level understanding of how products interact with natural, social, and economic systems across their entire life cycle.

The traditional model of product design is 'cradle-to-grave': raw materials are extracted, manufactured into products, used, and then discarded — a linear flow that terminates in landfill or incineration. The cradle-to-cradle (C2C) model, articulated by architect William McDonough and chemist Michael Braungart in their 2002 book 'Cradle to Cradle: Remaking the Way We Make Things', proposes instead that products should be designed so that all materials cycle perpetually through two closed loops: the biological cycle (biodegradable materials return to the soil, providing nutrients for new biological growth) and the technical cycle (synthetic and mineral materials are maintained at their highest quality level indefinitely through reuse, remanufacturing, and recycling). C2C certification evaluates products against five categories: material health (absence of hazardous substances), material reutilisation (recyclable or compostable), renewable energy, water stewardship, and social fairness.

Design for Environment (DfE, also called Eco-design or Environmentally Conscious Design) is a set of design strategies that reduce environmental impact at each stage of the product life cycle. Key DfE strategies include: Design for Disassembly (making products easy to take apart for material recovery); Design for Recyclability (using materials with established recycling streams, minimising material mixing); Design for Longevity (durable design, modular and repairable architecture, software updates); Design for Reduced Energy Use (efficient components, standby modes, thermal optimisation); Design for Use of Renewable/Recycled Materials; and Design for Reduced Packaging (minimising packaging weight and volume, using recyclable materials).

Material selection is one of the most impactful decisions in sustainable design. Each material has different embodied energy (the total energy consumed in its extraction, processing, and manufacture), carbon footprint, toxicity profile, recyclability, and supply security. Life cycle assessment (LCA) quantifies these impacts across the product life cycle. In the absence of full LCA data, designers can use material selection databases (CES EduPack, Granta) to compare materials on sustainability metrics. Substituting high-impact materials (virgin aluminium from bauxite mining) with lower-impact alternatives (recycled aluminium, or in some cases, natural fibre composites) can substantially reduce product environmental footprint.

Ecolabelling schemes provide consumers and specifiers with independently verified information about products' environmental credentials. The EU Ecolabel (Flower label), Energy Star, FSC (timber), Marine Stewardship Council (MSC, seafood), and the Cradle to Cradle Certified mark are examples. Type I ecolabels (ISO 14024) involve third-party verification against defined criteria. Type III environmental declarations (ISO 14025, Environmental Product Declarations — EPDs) disclose quantitative lifecycle data without making comparative claims. Ecolabels can drive market demand for more sustainable products and incentivise manufacturers to improve performance to meet certification criteria. Limitations include: greenwashing risk (unverified claims), proliferation of conflicting schemes confusing consumers, and criteria that may not reflect the most significant impacts.

quiz.question: Explain the cradle-to-cradle design concept and describe how it differs from the conventional cradle-to-grave model.
quiz.markscheme: Cradle-to-grave: linear model — raw materials extracted, manufactured, used, then discarded to landfill or incineration; material value is permanently lost. Cradle-to-cradle (McDonough and Braungart): all materials cycle perpetually through two closed loops — the biological cycle (biodegradable materials return to soil as nutrients) and the technical cycle (synthetic/mineral materials are recovered and maintained at highest quality through reuse, remanufacturing, recycling). The key distinction is that C2C eliminates the concept of 'waste' — every material output is an input to another cycle. This requires designing products so materials can be cleanly separated and recovered.

quiz.question: Describe three Design for Environment (DfE) strategies and explain how each reduces environmental impact.
quiz.markscheme: Accept any three: Design for Disassembly (snap-fits, screws instead of adhesives, material labelling) — enables material recovery and recycling at end of life; Design for Longevity (durable materials, repairable architecture, modular components) — extends product life, reducing replacement frequency and thus resource consumption; Design for Recyclability (mono-materials, avoiding mixed-material composites, using materials with established recycling streams) — increases recyclate quality and quantity; Design for Reduced Energy Use (efficient components, sleep modes, thermal insulation) — reduces use-phase energy and associated carbon emissions; Design for Reduced Packaging — less material and transport energy. Each strategy should be explained with a clear mechanism.

quiz.question: Analyse how embodied energy and end-of-life recyclability should both be considered when selecting a material for a consumer product casing.
quiz.markscheme: Embodied energy is the total energy consumed in extracting, processing, and manufacturing a material — higher embodied energy = greater carbon footprint at production. For a casing, aluminium has high embodied energy (primary production) but can be recycled with ~5% of the virgin energy — if the product is likely to be recycled, lifecycle embodied energy is much lower. ABS plastic has lower primary embodied energy but lower recycling rates and a fossil fuel origin. Polypropylene may have better recyclability within established plastic recycling streams. The choice should consider: will the product be effectively collected for recycling in the target market? Is the recycling infrastructure available? The trade-off between production impact and end-of-life recovery must be analysed together using available LCA data.

quiz.question: Evaluate the effectiveness of ecolabelling schemes in promoting sustainable product design.
quiz.markscheme: Strengths: ecolabels provide independently verified environmental information, enabling informed consumer and specifier choices; they create market incentives for manufacturers to improve performance to meet certification criteria; they raise awareness of sustainability issues. Limitations: proliferation of competing schemes (FSC, EU Ecolabel, Rainforest Alliance, Energy Star, etc.) confuses consumers; criteria may not cover all significant impacts; greenwashing risk from self-declared claims without third-party verification; consumer awareness and willingness to pay for certified products varies significantly; labels cannot fully capture complex lifecycle trade-offs in a single symbol. Overall: ecolabels are a useful but imperfect tool — most effective when combined with regulation, transparency in criteria, and strong consumer environmental literacy.

quiz.question: Discuss how the principle of design for longevity contributes to sustainable design, including potential limitations.
quiz.markscheme: Design for longevity reduces the frequency at which products must be manufactured and disposed of, directly reducing resource consumption and waste generation per year of service. Strategies include: durable materials and construction; modular architecture allowing component replacement rather than whole-product replacement; repairability (accessible, documented, with available spare parts); software updates extending functional life of digital products; timeless aesthetic reducing premature disposal driven by fashion. Limitations: very long-lived products may not benefit from improved efficiency of newer models (an energy-inefficient appliance used for 30 years may have higher total energy use than replacement with a more efficient model after 10 years); some products genuinely improve so rapidly that longevity conflicts with technology advancement; higher manufacturing cost of durable products may be inaccessible to lower-income consumers. A full discussion weighs these trade-offs.

title: Life cycle analysis (LCA)
objectives: Define life cycle analysis (LCA) and describe its four phases according to ISO 14040/44.
Explain the concept of system boundary in LCA and describe how it is defined.
Describe the main impact categories assessed in LCA.
Analyse the application of LCA to a specific product design decision.
Discuss the limitations of LCA as a tool for sustainable design decision-making.
summary:
Life cycle analysis (LCA), also called life cycle assessment, is a systematic methodology for quantifying the environmental impacts associated with all stages of a product's life — from raw material extraction through materials processing, manufacture, distribution, use, and end-of-life treatment. LCA enables designers and manufacturers to identify the most environmentally significant stages of a product's life and to make evidence-based decisions about where to focus improvement efforts. It is standardised by ISO 14040 (Principles and Framework) and ISO 14044 (Requirements and Guidelines).

ISO 14040/44 defines four phases of LCA. Goal and Scope Definition: the purpose of the study is defined, including the functional unit (the unit of output to which all impacts are referenced — e.g. 'one wash cycle' for a washing machine rather than 'one machine'), the system boundary (what is included in and excluded from the analysis), and the intended audience. Inventory Analysis (Life Cycle Inventory, LCI): all inputs (energy, materials, water) and outputs (emissions, waste) are quantified for every process within the system boundary — this is data-intensive and is the most time-consuming phase. Impact Assessment (LCIA): the inventory flows are translated into environmental impact scores across defined impact categories (e.g. global warming potential, acidification, eutrophication, water depletion). Interpretation: results are analysed to identify significant issues, check consistency with the goal and scope, and draw conclusions and recommendations.

The system boundary is a critical LCA parameter that defines what stages and processes are included in the study. Common boundary definitions: 'cradle-to-grave' includes all stages from raw material extraction to end-of-life disposal; 'cradle-to-gate' includes only up to the factory gate (not use or disposal); 'gate-to-gate' covers only the manufacturing step; 'cradle-to-cradle' accounts for material recovery and recycling. System boundary choices significantly affect results — a cradle-to-gate study may miss the dominant impact category (often the use phase for energy-consuming products). Boundary choices must be justified and transparent.

LCA impact categories include: Global Warming Potential (GWP — kg CO₂ equivalent, the most widely reported metric); Acidification Potential (sulphur and nitrogen oxides damaging ecosystems and buildings); Eutrophication Potential (nutrient runoff to water bodies — algal blooms, oxygen depletion); Water Depletion; Ozone Depletion Potential; Particulate Matter Formation; Land Use; and Toxicity categories (human toxicity, ecotoxicity). The choice of which impact categories to report affects the conclusions: a product with low carbon emissions may have high water depletion impact, so single-metric comparisons (e.g. carbon footprint alone) can be misleading.

LCA has significant limitations. Data quality and availability: LCA results are only as good as the inventory data, which varies enormously in quality and is often estimated or taken from databases with average rather than product-specific values. Uncertainty: LCA results have large uncertainty ranges that are often not reported alongside results. Subjectivity: the choice of system boundary, functional unit, and impact weighting all involve value judgements that affect outcomes. LCA does not capture social impacts or costs. LCA studies are expensive and time-consuming, making them impractical for everyday design decisions — simplified screening tools (material comparison databases, CO₂ calculators) are used as proxies.

quiz.question: Define the functional unit in LCA and explain why it is important for comparing two alternative products.
quiz.markscheme: The functional unit is the unit of service or performance to which all environmental impacts in an LCA are referenced — it defines what is being compared. For example, for washing machine comparison, the functional unit might be 'washing 1 kg of laundry to a standard clean' rather than 'one washing machine', because machines of different capacity and efficiency provide different amounts of service per unit. Comparing machine A and machine B per kilogram of laundry washed (over lifetime) gives a fair comparison; comparing per machine would favour smaller or shorter-lived machines. The functional unit must be defined carefully so that comparisons are fair and relevant to the design decision.

quiz.question: Describe the four phases of LCA according to ISO 14040/44.
quiz.markscheme: Goal and Scope Definition: defines the purpose, functional unit, system boundary, and audience. Inventory Analysis (LCI): quantifies all material and energy inputs and all emissions and waste outputs for every process within the system boundary. Impact Assessment (LCIA): translates inventory flows into environmental impact scores across defined categories (e.g. GWP, acidification, water use). Interpretation: analyses results to identify significant hotspots, checks consistency, draws conclusions, and makes recommendations for design improvement or selection between alternatives. Students should describe each phase, not just name it.

quiz.question: Explain how the choice of system boundary affects the conclusions of an LCA study.
quiz.markscheme: The system boundary defines what stages and processes are included. A cradle-to-gate study (extraction to factory gate) will miss use-phase impacts — for energy-consuming products like appliances, the use phase typically dominates lifetime impact, so a cradle-to-gate study would systematically understate total impact and could lead to incorrect comparative conclusions. Including or excluding end-of-life recycling significantly changes results for materials-intensive products where recycling credit is large. Boundary choices must be disclosed and justified — undisclosed narrow boundaries (e.g. excluding high-impact stages) can be used selectively to make a product appear more sustainable than it is (a form of greenwashing).

quiz.question: Analyse how an LCA of a reusable cotton shopping bag versus a single-use plastic bag reveals the complexity of environmental comparison.
quiz.markscheme: A cotton bag has high production impact: cotton cultivation is water- and pesticide-intensive; textile manufacturing is energy-intensive. A single-use LDPE bag has much lower production impact per bag. However, the comparison depends critically on: how many times each bag is reused (cotton bags typically require 50-150 uses to break even on production impact vs single use plastic, depending on impact category); the end-of-life treatment of the plastic bag (recycled vs landfill vs littered in environment — plastic litter has severe ecosystem impacts not captured in standard LCA categories); and which impact category is prioritised (GWP, water use, or plastic pollution). This example illustrates that LCA conclusions are sensitive to assumptions and impact category choice — a single-metric comparison can be highly misleading.

quiz.question: Discuss two significant limitations of LCA as a tool for supporting sustainable design decisions.
quiz.markscheme: Data quality and availability: LCA inventory data is often estimated, drawn from generic industry averages, or unavailable for specific processes and materials — particularly for supply chains in developing countries. Results depend heavily on data quality; two LCAs of the same product using different databases can produce very different results. Scope limitations: LCA does not capture social impacts (labour conditions, community impacts) or economic impacts — it is an environmental tool only. These non-environmental dimensions may be equally or more important in a given decision context. Additionally: the expertise, time, and cost of a full ISO LCA are prohibitive for most design projects; uncertainty in results is often not communicated to decision-makers; and comparisons between products with different functions are only valid with carefully chosen functional units. Students should explain each limitation and its practical implication.

title: Green design
objectives: Define green design and distinguish it from broader sustainable design.
Describe the key strategies of green design including material efficiency, energy efficiency, and reduced toxicity.
Explain the role of legislation and regulation in driving green design.
Analyse the use of biomimicry and natural systems as inspiration for green design solutions.
Evaluate the challenges and trade-offs involved in implementing green design in commercial products.
summary:
Green design (or eco-design) focuses specifically on reducing the environmental impact of products — primarily in terms of material and energy efficiency, toxicity reduction, and end-of-life management. It is sometimes distinguished from the broader concept of sustainable design, which also incorporates social and economic dimensions, though in practice the terms are often used interchangeably. Green design is most precisely understood as the application of environmental thinking to specific product design decisions, guided by methodologies such as LCA and DfE.

The key strategies of green design include: material efficiency (using the minimum quantity of material required to meet functional requirements — through structural optimisation, lightweighting, and reduced processing waste); energy efficiency in manufacture and use (selecting energy-efficient manufacturing processes, and designing products to consume minimum energy during operation); reduced toxicity (substituting hazardous substances with benign alternatives — driven in Europe by the REACH regulation and the RoHS directive, which restrict hazardous chemicals in electronics); designing for end-of-life management (recyclability, compostability, remanufacturability); and extending product life through durability and repairability.

Legislation and regulation are powerful drivers of green design, often more effective than voluntary initiatives in achieving widespread market change. The EU has developed a comprehensive regulatory framework: the Ecodesign Regulation (originally for energy-related products, now expanding to a wider range of products under the Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation — ESPR) sets mandatory minimum energy efficiency, repairability, and recyclability standards; the WEEE Directive requires manufacturers to fund collection and recycling of waste electrical and electronic equipment; the RoHS Directive restricts hazardous substances in electronics (lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium); the REACH Regulation manages chemicals of very high concern; and packaging regulations require recycled content and recyclability. Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) schemes across product categories financially incentivise improved end-of-life design.

Biomimicry — drawing design inspiration from natural systems and organisms — was discussed in Topic 2.4 but is particularly relevant to green design because natural systems are inherently 'green': they operate on solar energy, produce no persistent waste (one organism's waste is another's food), and have been optimised through evolution for material and energy efficiency. Natural structures such as bone (hierarchical architecture combining stiffness and lightness), bamboo (hollow tubular structure combining strength and minimal material), nacre (mother of pearl — layered ceramic-polymer structure of exceptional toughness), and the gecko's toe pads (dry adhesion through van der Waals forces — no chemical adhesive) have all inspired engineering and design innovations.

Implementing green design in commercial products involves trade-offs and challenges. Recycled and bio-based materials may cost more than virgin conventional materials; design for disassembly may increase manufacturing complexity; reducing packaging may increase product damage rates in distribution; designing for longevity may reduce replacement sales. Companies face competitive pressure that can work against green choices: if a competitor uses cheaper, less sustainable materials, their product costs less. This is the market failure at the heart of green design — environmental costs are often externalised (not borne by the producer or consumer) and therefore not reflected in price. Regulatory requirements, carbon pricing, and sustainability certification can correct this market failure by internalising environmental costs.

quiz.question: Define green design and describe three key strategies used by designers to reduce environmental impact.
quiz.markscheme: Green design is the practice of designing products to minimise environmental impact across their life cycle. Key strategies: material efficiency (using the minimum material required — lightweighting, structural optimisation, reducing off-cuts); energy efficiency (in manufacture and use — efficient components, standby modes, thermal optimisation); reduced toxicity (substituting hazardous substances with benign alternatives — meeting REACH/RoHS requirements); designing for end-of-life (recyclability, compostability); extending product life (durability, repairability, modular replacement). Each strategy should be described with a clear mechanism and an example.

quiz.question: Explain how the EU RoHS Directive has driven green design in the electronics industry.
quiz.markscheme: RoHS (Restriction of Hazardous Substances) Directive restricts specified hazardous substances in electrical and electronic equipment: lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium, and certain flame retardants. This is a mandatory regulatory standard — products cannot be sold in the EU without compliance. It drove electronics manufacturers to: reformulate solder alloys (lead-free solder replacing tin-lead); find alternative surface finishes (replacing lead-tin coatings on components); reformulate PCB laminates (replacing brominated flame retardants); and substitute cadmium-based pigments. The regulation affected the entire global supply chain (not only EU manufacturers, but all suppliers worldwide who want to sell to the EU market) and achieved near-universal adoption of lead-free electronics. This is an example of regulation achieving an environmental outcome that voluntary action had not.

quiz.question: Describe one example of biomimicry applied to green design and explain the environmental benefit it provides.
quiz.markscheme: Accept one with both parts: lotus leaf effect (superhydrophobic self-cleaning surfaces inspired by the micro/nanostructure of the lotus leaf surface — applied to building materials, textiles, and glass that repel water and dirt without chemical cleaning agents, reducing the environmental impact of cleaning); bone-inspired lattice structures in 3D-printed parts (hierarchical porous architecture combining high strength with low material use — reduces material consumption and lightens structures); gecko-inspired dry adhesives (van der Waals adhesion without chemical adhesives — enables reversible bonding without toxic solvents or adhesive waste). The environmental benefit should be explicitly stated.

quiz.question: Analyse why economic competitive pressure can work against green design choices, and identify one policy mechanism that can correct this.
quiz.markscheme: If environmental costs are externalised — not reflected in the price of materials, energy, or disposal — then cheaper, less sustainable choices appear financially superior. A competitor using virgin plastics, cheap disposal, and no end-of-life responsibility will have lower costs than one investing in recycled materials and take-back schemes. This is a market failure: the full social and environmental cost is not borne by the producer. Policy corrections: carbon pricing (making CO₂ emissions financially costly, reflecting their social cost); Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR, making producers bear end-of-life costs, incentivising design for recyclability); minimum standards regulation (removing less sustainable products from the market, levelling the playing field); and eco-design standards (requiring minimum energy efficiency and repairability, preventing competitive advantage from poor environmental performance).

quiz.question: Evaluate the trade-offs involved in choosing bio-based materials for product components in green design.
quiz.markscheme: Potential advantages: lower fossil fuel dependency; potential for compostability or biodegradability at end of life; lower lifecycle carbon if sustainably grown; renewable supply. Trade-offs and limitations: bio-based materials may compete with food production for agricultural land (food vs fuel dilemma); sustainable agricultural practices must be verified (unsustainable biomass production can cause deforestation and biodiversity loss — a worse outcome than fossil-based alternatives); bio-based does not mean biodegradable in all environments (PLA requires industrial composting to degrade — will not biodegrade in marine or landfill environments); performance may be inferior to conventional alternatives; cost is typically higher. A robust evaluation would acknowledge both the potential and the specific conditions under which bio-based materials genuinely reduce environmental impact, noting that the answer is context-dependent.

title: The market
objectives: Define a market and describe the main types of markets relevant to product design.
Explain supply and demand and how price is determined in a market.
Describe market segmentation and explain why it is used.
Analyse how market research informs product development decisions.
Discuss the factors that determine market attractiveness for a new product.
summary:
A market is any mechanism by which buyers and sellers exchange goods, services, or information — it need not be a physical place. For product designers and manufacturers, understanding the market in which a product will compete is essential: market size, structure, growth rate, customer characteristics, competitive dynamics, and pricing all shape the commercial viability and design requirements of a new product.

Markets are commonly classified by the type of customer: business-to-consumer (B2C) markets involve selling to individual consumers for personal use; business-to-business (B2B) markets involve selling to organisations that use products in their own production or operations; and business-to-government (B2G) markets involve selling to public sector bodies. Within these broad categories, markets can be further classified by product category (automotive, consumer electronics, healthcare, etc.), geographic region, or distribution channel.

The basic economic model of supply and demand describes how price is determined in a market. Demand for a product increases as price falls (demand curve slopes downward); supply increases as price rises (supply curve slopes upward). The market price settles at the equilibrium where quantity demanded equals quantity supplied. Understanding price elasticity of demand — how sensitive demand is to price changes — is important for pricing strategy: luxury goods have inelastic demand (price changes have little effect on quantity demanded); commodity goods have elastic demand (small price rises lead to large demand reductions).

Market segmentation is the process of dividing a market into groups of potential buyers who share similar needs, characteristics, or behaviours, so that a company can target specific segments with tailored products and marketing. Segmentation criteria include: demographic (age, gender, income, education — e.g. a premium children's educational toy targets high-income parents); geographic (country, region, urban/rural); psychographic (lifestyle, values, personality — e.g. environmentally conscious consumers); and behavioural (usage rate, brand loyalty, benefit sought). Effective segmentation allows designers and manufacturers to focus resources on segments where their offering can provide the greatest value, rather than trying to serve all customers with a single undifferentiated product.

Market research is the systematic collection and analysis of information about target markets, customers, competitors, and the external environment. Primary research involves gathering new data directly (surveys, interviews, focus groups, observation, usability testing). Secondary research involves analysing existing data (industry reports, government statistics, published research). Market research informs product development decisions by: confirming or challenging assumptions about user needs; providing insight into willingness to pay; mapping the competitive landscape; identifying unserved segments; and providing evidence to support investment decisions.

Market attractiveness for a new product is determined by multiple factors: market size (larger markets offer more revenue opportunity); market growth rate (growing markets are easier to enter as there is demand for new entrants); competitive intensity (crowded, commodity markets are harder to enter profitably); barriers to entry (high barriers — regulatory, technical, or capital requirements — protect existing competitors but also protect new entrants once established); and customer switching costs (high switching costs make it harder to attract customers from existing suppliers).

quiz.question: Define market segmentation and explain two bases on which a manufacturer of outdoor sports equipment might segment its market.
quiz.markscheme: Market segmentation divides a market into groups of buyers with similar needs or characteristics. For outdoor sports equipment: Behavioural — by activity type (rock climbing, kayaking, hiking, skiing) and skill level (beginner/recreational vs. professional/competition) — allows tailoring of product specification and price to specific activity requirements; Psychographic — by lifestyle and values (e.g. environmentally conscious consumers who want sustainable materials and supply chains) — allows specific product positioning and material/manufacturing choices; Demographic — by age and income (e.g. premium segment targeting affluent, experienced adults; budget segment targeting young, first-time participants). Each basis should be explained with a specific application.

quiz.question: Explain the concept of price elasticity of demand and describe its implications for pricing a new consumer electronic product.
quiz.markscheme: Price elasticity of demand measures how responsive quantity demanded is to a price change: elastic demand means a small price rise causes a large fall in demand (common in commodity or highly competitive markets); inelastic demand means price rises have little effect on quantity demanded (common in luxury goods, essential products, or products with no close substitutes). For a new consumer electronic product: if it is differentiated and has strong brand equity or unique features, demand may be inelastic at launch (price skimming strategy — high launch price captures maximum revenue from enthusiastic early adopters). As the market matures and competition intensifies, demand becomes more elastic, requiring price reductions (market penetration). Understanding elasticity helps set optimal price at each PLC stage.

quiz.question: Describe the difference between primary and secondary market research and explain when a product development team would use each.
quiz.markscheme: Primary research: collecting new data directly from target users or the market — surveys, interviews, focus groups, usability testing, observation. Used when: specific questions about user needs, preferences, or behaviours need to be answered that existing data cannot address; when validating assumptions about the target market; when testing design concepts with users. Secondary research: analysing existing data — industry reports, academic studies, government statistics, competitor analysis. Used when: understanding overall market size, growth, and structure; identifying known competitors and their positioning; gathering background context before designing primary research. Primary research is more expensive and time-consuming but yields tailored, current data; secondary research is cheaper but may not answer specific design questions.

quiz.question: Analyse how Porter's Five Forces model could be used to assess the market attractiveness for a new entrant into the electric bicycle market.
quiz.markscheme: Porter's Five Forces: Threat of new entrants (barriers to entry — capital requirements for tooling and manufacturing are moderate; brand building takes time; some technology IP may be held by incumbents; barriers are moderate overall, allowing new entrants); Competitive rivalry (growing market, currently medium intensity but increasing rapidly as the market grows and attracts new players); Supplier power (components — batteries, motors, frames — sourced from multiple suppliers globally, moderate power); Buyer power (many buyers, low individual power; however, increasingly sophisticated buyers comparing specifications and price as the market matures); Threat of substitutes (conventional bicycles, public transport, e-scooters — significant threat in urban markets). Analysis should conclude with an overall attractiveness assessment for a new entrant.

quiz.question: Discuss how thorough market research before a product development project can reduce commercial risk.
quiz.markscheme: Market research before development: identifies whether the proposed product actually addresses a real user need (reducing the risk of developing a product nobody wants); validates willingness to pay (ensuring the product can be priced at a level that covers costs and earns return); maps the competitive landscape (preventing investment in a market already dominated by entrenched competitors); identifies regulatory requirements early (avoiding costly late-stage design changes to meet standards); and sizes the market (ensuring it is large enough to justify investment). Without market research, teams risk developing technically excellent products that fail commercially because they are solving the wrong problem, at the wrong price point, for the wrong market. The cost of market research is typically a fraction of development cost and can save multiples of that by preventing poor investment decisions.

title: Marketing mix
objectives: Define the marketing mix and identify its elements (the 7Ps).
Explain how each element of the marketing mix is relevant to a new product launch.
Describe the role of packaging design as a component of the product element.
Analyse how the marketing mix should be adapted for different market segments.
Discuss how digital channels have changed the promotion and place elements of the marketing mix.
summary:
The marketing mix is the set of controllable tactical tools that a company uses to produce the desired response in its target market. Originally formulated as the 4Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion), the framework was extended to 7Ps to better accommodate services and contemporary marketing practice: the additional three are People, Process, and Physical Evidence.

Product refers to the physical design, features, quality, branding, and packaging of the offering. For a manufactured product, the product element encompasses everything from materials and performance specifications to after-sales service, warranty, and the product's role in a wider brand portfolio. Design decisions directly determine the product element — form, function, materials, ergonomics, aesthetics, and packaging are all product mix variables. Packaging serves multiple functions simultaneously: protecting the product in transit and retail, communicating brand identity and product information, differentiating on shelf, and increasingly signalling sustainability credentials (minimal, recyclable packaging).

Price is not simply the cost-plus calculation but a strategic variable that signals quality, positions the product relative to competitors, and determines accessibility to different market segments. Pricing strategies include cost-plus pricing (adding a margin to cost), value-based pricing (setting price relative to perceived customer value), skimming (high launch price to capture early adopters, then reducing), penetration pricing (low launch price to gain market share rapidly), and psychological pricing (£9.99 rather than £10). Price must be consistent with other elements of the mix — premium product positioning supported by premium packaging and selective distribution is undermined by discounted pricing.

Place (distribution) refers to how and where the product is made available to customers. Distribution channels include direct-to-consumer (company website, own stores), retail (supermarkets, specialist retailers, department stores), wholesale (distributors selling to retailers), and increasingly online marketplaces (Amazon, Tmall). The choice of channel affects accessibility, price, brand perception, and the ability to control the customer experience. Distribution design is increasingly recognised as a source of competitive advantage.

Promotion encompasses all communications with customers and potential customers: advertising (mass media, digital, out-of-home), public relations (earned media, press coverage), direct marketing, social media, content marketing, search engine optimisation, influencer marketing, and sales promotion (discounts, competitions). The rise of digital channels has fragmented the media landscape — consumers are exposed to thousands of marketing messages daily across multiple channels, making cut-through increasingly difficult. Effective promotional strategy requires selecting channels where the target segment is most receptive.

People, Process, and Physical Evidence are particularly relevant for services but also apply to manufactured products: the people who sell and support a product shape the customer experience; the process of purchasing, delivery, and after-sales service affects satisfaction; and physical evidence (the retail environment, unboxing experience, product documentation quality) reinforces brand perception. The 7P framework encourages designers to think beyond the product itself to the complete customer experience.

quiz.question: Identify the 7Ps of the marketing mix and explain briefly what each element encompasses.
quiz.markscheme: Product: the physical design, features, quality, branding, packaging, and associated services. Price: the pricing strategy, list price, discounts, and payment terms. Place: distribution channels and locations where the product is available. Promotion: all communication activities (advertising, PR, social media, sales promotion). People: the staff who interact with customers and their role in shaping the experience. Process: the systems and processes by which the product/service is delivered. Physical evidence: tangible cues that provide evidence of quality — packaging, retail environment, documentation. All 7 should be identified and briefly described.

quiz.question: Explain two functions of packaging design in the marketing mix, giving examples.
quiz.markscheme: Accept any two: Protection (physical protection of the product during manufacture, transport, retail, and use — e.g. blister packaging for small electronics components; cushioned boxing for fragile goods); Communication (communicating brand identity, product information, usage instructions, and legal requirements — e.g. Apple's minimalist white packaging signals premium quality and simplicity); Differentiation on shelf (distinctive shape, colour, or material that makes the product stand out in retail environment — e.g. the distinctive Coca-Cola bottle shape); Sustainability signalling (minimal, recyclable, or bio-based packaging communicates environmental values — e.g. Lush cosmetics' minimal/solid packaging). Each function should be explained with a specific example.

quiz.question: Analyse how the marketing mix should be adapted when targeting a product at a premium market segment versus a budget market segment.
quiz.markscheme: Premium segment: Product — higher specification materials, superior finish, extended warranty, premium unboxing experience; Price — higher price reinforcing perceived quality (price signals value); Place — selective distribution through specialist retailers or direct brand stores, not mass market; Promotion — brand-image advertising in aspirational contexts, influencer endorsement, editorial coverage. Budget segment: Product — simplified specification, adequate performance, minimal extras; Price — low price as primary purchasing driver, competitive pricing, price promotions; Place — mass market distribution (supermarkets, online marketplaces) for maximum accessibility; Promotion — promotional advertising emphasising value-for-money, price comparison sites, direct response. Consistency across the mix is critical — a premium product in a budget distribution channel undermines brand positioning.

quiz.question: Describe how digital channels have transformed the promotion element of the marketing mix.
quiz.markscheme: Digital channels have: fragmented the media landscape (audiences dispersed across social media platforms, streaming services, and devices — mass TV advertising less effective); enabled highly targeted advertising (data-driven targeting by demographics, interests, and behaviour); created new promotional formats (content marketing, influencer marketing, user-generated content, viral campaigns); enabled direct-to-consumer communication without media intermediaries; provided real-time performance measurement (click-through rates, conversion rates, ROI immediately measurable — unlike traditional advertising); and shifted power towards consumers who can instantly share reviews and reactions globally. Challenges include: advertising saturation (consumers develop 'banner blindness'); ad blockers; privacy regulation (GDPR) restricting data use; and the need for authentic, engaging content in a highly competitive attention environment.

quiz.question: Discuss how the place element of the marketing mix has changed for consumer electronics manufacturers with the growth of e-commerce.
quiz.markscheme: Traditional distribution: manufacturers → distributors → retailers → consumers (multi-step, with each step adding cost and margin; retailers control shelf space and product presentation). E-commerce changes: direct-to-consumer (D2C) enabled — manufacturers can sell via own website, bypassing retailer margin and controlling brand experience fully; online marketplaces (Amazon, Alibaba) provide access to global customers without physical retail infrastructure; subscription and software distribution models relevant to software-enabled products; product demonstration challenge — consumers cannot physically handle before purchase (partially offset by online reviews, video demos, and liberal return policies). Trade-offs: D2C requires investment in e-commerce platform, logistics, and customer service; retail partnerships provide physical demonstration space, impulse purchase, and trust from the retailer's brand association. Most manufacturers now operate hybrid multichannel strategies.

title: Product launch and promotion
objectives: Describe the key activities involved in launching a new product to market.
Explain how promotional strategies differ between B2C and B2B markets.
Describe the role of public relations and earned media in product launch.
Analyse how product launch timing affects commercial success.
Discuss the role of trade shows and exhibitions in product promotion.
summary:
A product launch is the coordinated set of activities by which a new product is introduced to its target market. A successful launch generates awareness, creates desire, converts initial interest into purchase, and establishes the product's position in the competitive landscape. The launch is typically one of the most resource-intensive phases of the product's commercial life, and the decisions made in planning the launch significantly affect the product's initial trajectory.
