Key launch activities include: finalising product and packaging to launch specification; establishing supply chain readiness (ensuring adequate production capacity and stock); training sales teams and channel partners on product features and selling propositions; creating marketing communications materials (advertising, website content, product videos, press releases, point-of-sale displays); preparing customer service and support infrastructure; and managing media and influencer briefings ahead of public announcement. The launch event itself — a press conference, a live demonstration, an online announcement — is designed to generate concentrated media attention and social sharing that amplifies the launch signal.

Promotion strategies differ significantly between B2C and B2B markets. B2C product launch typically involves mass media advertising (TV, digital, social media), influencer partnerships, retail point-of-sale display, and consumer PR to generate broad awareness and emotional appeal quickly. Purchase decisions are often made quickly and may be impulsive; emotional resonance and brand appeal are important drivers. B2B product launch focuses on professional media coverage (trade press, LinkedIn, industry publications), targeted direct marketing to qualified prospects, participation in industry trade shows and conferences, technical demonstrations, and relationship-based selling. B2B purchase decisions involve multiple stakeholders, longer consideration periods, and are primarily rational (ROI-focused). Sales teams play a more prominent role than in B2C.

Public relations (PR) and earned media are particularly valuable at launch because they carry the credibility of third-party endorsement that paid advertising lacks. A favourable review in a respected publication or a positive social media post from an influential user can drive more awareness and trust than equivalent advertising spend. PR activities include: providing product samples to journalists and content creators before launch; organising press events; distributing press releases with embargo dates that synchronise media coverage; and briefing key analysts and opinion formers. Managing the PR programme requires careful coordination to ensure consistent messaging across multiple media simultaneously.

Launch timing significantly affects commercial success. Launching too early (before the product is ready, the supply chain is reliable, or the market is ready to adopt) risks a poor initial experience for early users that sets a negative reputation; launching too late allows competitors to occupy the market position first or allows the opportunity window to close. Seasonal timing affects certain product categories (Christmas launches for consumer products, academic year launches for educational products, spring launches for outdoor products). In fast-moving technology markets, product generations have defined launch windows — missing them can mean waiting 12-24 months for the next suitable cycle.

Trade shows and exhibitions provide opportunities to demonstrate products directly to industry buyers, media, and partners in a concentrated setting. Industry-specific trade shows (CES for consumer electronics, Bauma for construction equipment, Automechanika for automotive components) concentrate the target audience and facilitate direct comparison with competitors. Trade shows are particularly important for B2B product launches where personal demonstration and relationship-building are essential, and for products where physical experience (touch, feel, scale) is central to the purchasing decision.

quiz.question: Describe three key activities that must be completed before a new product launch.
quiz.markscheme: Accept any three: finalising product and packaging to launch specification; establishing supply chain readiness — ensuring adequate production capacity and stock to meet anticipated demand; training sales teams and channel partners on product features, competitive positioning, and selling propositions; creating marketing communications materials (advertising, website, videos, press releases, point-of-sale); preparing customer service and support infrastructure; conducting media and influencer briefings ahead of announcement; establishing distribution agreements. Each activity should be described with a clear explanation of why it is necessary before launch.

quiz.question: Explain how promotional strategies for a new industrial machine differ from those for a new consumer product.
quiz.markscheme: Industrial machine (B2B): promotional strategy focuses on trade press and technical publications; direct outreach to procurement, engineering, and management decision-makers; participation in industry trade shows; technical demonstrations; detailed specification sheets and ROI case studies; relationship-based selling (long sales cycles, multiple decision-makers). Emotional and brand appeal are less important than technical performance, total cost of ownership, and service/support capability. Consumer product (B2C): mass advertising via digital, social media, TV; influencer partnerships; retail POS; consumer PR generating reviews; emotional and lifestyle positioning. Purchase decisions faster, often by one individual; brand appeal and emotional resonance important.

quiz.question: Analyse why favourable earned media coverage (e.g. from a respected reviewer) is considered more valuable than equivalent paid advertising at a product launch.
quiz.markscheme: Earned media carries third-party credibility — the reviewer is independent and perceived as objective. Consumers trust product reviews from credible journalists and users significantly more than brand advertising. Advertising is known to be paid for by the brand; reviews are perceived as independent. Favourable earned coverage also reaches engaged, relevant audiences (readers of a specific technology publication are highly relevant to a tech product launch); it persists online and can be shared; and it can be referenced and quoted in subsequent marketing materials. However, earned media cannot be fully controlled — negative coverage carries equivalent credibility and can damage the launch. Managing PR therefore involves careful product preparation and media relationship cultivation.

quiz.question: Discuss the risks of launching a product before its supply chain is fully ready to meet demand.
quiz.markscheme: If a product launch generates strong demand but supply is insufficient: customers who cannot obtain the product may buy a competitor's instead; initial frustrated customers may post negative reviews; excessive stock-outs damage retailer relationships and shelf presence; product scalping (secondary market at inflated prices) may frustrate genuine customers and harm brand perception; and after strong launch momentum may dissipate before stock is available. Conversely, over-preparing supply adds inventory cost and write-down risk if demand is lower than forecast. Launch planning requires careful demand forecasting, production ramp-up coordination, and safety stock planning. The risks are particularly acute for physical products with long manufacturing lead times.

quiz.question: Explain the role of trade shows in B2B product promotion and identify one advantage and one limitation.
quiz.markscheme: Trade shows concentrate the relevant professional audience (buyers, engineers, media, partners) in one location at one time, enabling direct product demonstration, relationship building, lead generation, and competitor comparison. They are particularly important for products requiring physical demonstration — scale, operation, finish, and sound/vibration characteristics that cannot be conveyed by specifications alone. Advantage: concentrated access to qualified prospects; direct comparison with competitors in a neutral setting; media and press coverage from a single event. Limitation: expensive (stand design, staff time, travel, accommodation) with uncertain ROI; lead generation quality varies; declining attendance at some established shows as digital channels increasingly serve information needs. The strategic value depends on the specific trade show's reach and the centrality of physical demonstration to the purchase decision.

title: Branding
objectives: Define a brand and distinguish it from a product or a logo.
Explain the concept of brand equity and describe how it is built over time.
Describe how product design contributes to brand identity.
Analyse the role of packaging design in communicating brand values.
Discuss the risks and benefits of brand extension strategies.
summary:
A brand is a name, term, design, symbol, or combination thereof that identifies the goods or services of one seller and differentiates them from those of competitors (AMA definition). More broadly, a brand is the sum of associations, perceptions, and emotions that consumers hold about a company or product — it exists in the minds of customers, not on the product itself. A logo or trademark is a component of brand identity, but the brand itself is the intangible perception of value, trust, and character that the identity represents. The distinction matters for designers: creating a distinctive visual identity (logo, colour, typography, form language) is a necessary but not sufficient condition for building a strong brand.

Brand equity is the premium value a brand adds to a product beyond its functional benefits — the extra price customers will pay, or the greater loyalty they demonstrate, because of the brand. Brand equity is built through: consistent quality and performance (the brand becomes trusted); sustained and consistent communications (reinforcing the associations consumers make with the brand over time); distinctive visual and aesthetic identity (the brand becomes immediately recognisable); strong distribution and point-of-sale presence; and positive consumer experiences at every touchpoint. Brand equity can be measured through consumer surveys, price premium analysis, and the premium value attributed to brand assets in financial transactions.

Product design is a primary carrier of brand identity — the form language, material choices, colour palette, and finish of physical products communicate brand values directly and powerfully. Apple's products are a pre-eminent example: the minimalist aesthetic, premium materials (aluminium, glass), restrained colour palette, and meticulous attention to detail across every product in the range communicate the brand's values of simplicity, quality, and innovation without a word of copy. Braun's design language (developed under Dieter Rams) is another classic example of design as brand carrier. Design consistency across the product portfolio is a key challenge: every new product must feel 'of the brand' while bringing something new.

Packaging design is often the first physical encounter a consumer has with a product. Packaging communicates brand values through: structural form (the shape and material of the container); graphic design (colour, typography, imagery); material choices (premium matte finishes, weight, texture); and the unboxing experience (the sequence in which the product is revealed and the tactile quality of internal materials). The 'unboxing' phenomenon, in which consumers photograph and share the experience of opening a new product, has elevated packaging to a significant brand communications medium — particularly for premium consumer electronics.

Brand extension is the strategy of using an established brand name to launch new products in different categories. A strong brand with positive associations in one category carries significant credibility and awareness into adjacent categories, reducing launch cost and risk. Examples: Virgin (airlines → rail → financial services → space tourism); Apple (computers → music players → phones → watches → services). Risks of brand extension: if the extension product underperforms, it damages the parent brand's associations of quality; extensions that are too far from the core brand can create confusion about what the brand stands for; managing brand consistency across very different product categories is challenging. The strongest brand extensions maintain a coherent brand idea across categories (Apple's consistent emphasis on premium design and simplicity), while the weakest dilute the brand by extending purely for commercial opportunism.

quiz.question: Define brand equity and explain how consistent product quality contributes to building it over time.
quiz.markscheme: Brand equity is the premium value a brand adds beyond a product's functional benefits — reflected in higher prices consumers will pay, stronger loyalty, and positive associations. Consistent quality contributes by: every positive experience reinforces the association of the brand with reliability and performance; repeated positive experiences build trust; trust reduces purchase risk, making consumers more likely to choose the brand again and to recommend it. If product quality is inconsistent, trust is damaged and brand equity erodes — each poor experience overrides multiple positive ones. Sustained quality over time is therefore the foundation of brand equity.

quiz.question: Explain how Apple uses product design to communicate brand identity across its product range.
quiz.markscheme: Apple's product design communicates brand identity through: a consistent design language across all products (minimalist forms, premium materials — aluminium, glass, ceramic — restrained colour palette, high-quality surface finish); the iconic Apple logo (always present, always in the same position); consistent interaction paradigms across software interfaces; carefully designed packaging (unboxing experience reinforces the premium positioning); retail environments designed to consistent standards (the Apple Store). These design elements collectively communicate the brand values of simplicity, premium quality, and innovation. The design consistency makes any Apple product instantly recognisable and creates a powerful brand experience that commands significant price premium over functionally comparable competitors.

quiz.question: Analyse the role of packaging design in communicating brand values, using a specific product as an example.
quiz.markscheme: Packaging is the first physical brand touchpoint for many consumers. Example: Lush Cosmetics — packaging strategy of selling many products 'naked' (without packaging) directly communicates the brand's environmental values; where packaging is used, it is recycled or recyclable and minimal. This packaging decision powerfully reinforces the brand's ethical and environmental positioning. Or Apple — minimal white/kraft packaging, premium matte finish, considered sequence of unboxing reinforces premium quality and simplicity values. In both cases, the packaging communicates without words the brand's core values. Students should describe both the specific design features of the packaging and the brand values they communicate.

quiz.question: Describe the risks of brand extension and explain how a company can mitigate them.
quiz.markscheme: Risks: if the extension product is of lower quality, it damages the parent brand's quality associations; overly wide extensions create confusion about the brand's meaning (what does this brand stand for if it sells everything?); the brand's associations may not transfer credibly to the new category; managing design and quality consistency across very different products is challenging. Mitigation: only extend into categories where the brand's core associations are relevant and transferable; maintain uncompromising quality standards in the extension; ensure the design language and brand expression are consistent; test extensions with core customers before launch; manage extensions under a sub-brand if there is significant category distance (e.g. Toyota Lexus — separate brand rather than 'Toyota premium cars').

quiz.question: Discuss how the 'unboxing' phenomenon has elevated the commercial importance of packaging design for consumer electronics brands.
quiz.markscheme: Unboxing videos and social media posts in which consumers share the experience of opening a new product have transformed packaging from a protection and information medium into a brand communications medium. An extraordinary unboxing experience — tactile premium materials, a considered reveal sequence, delightful details — generates user-created content that is shared with followers, amplifying the launch and reinforcing the premium brand perception. Apple's packaging is the canonical example — its deliberate friction, layered reveal, and premium matte surfaces create a ritualised experience that consumers want to share. This means that packaging investment generates marketing return beyond its traditional functions. Implication for designers: packaging design must be considered as a designed experience (a temporal, multisensory sequence) rather than simply a container. The commercial importance is particularly high for premium consumer electronics where brand premium is large and packaging cost is a small fraction of product value.

title: Just in time and just in case
objectives: Define just-in-time (JIT) production and explain its core principles.
Distinguish between JIT and just-in-case (JIC) inventory management.
Explain the role of supplier relationships in enabling JIT production.
Analyse the advantages and risks of JIT production systems.
Discuss the conditions under which JIT is and is not appropriate.
summary:
Just-in-time (JIT) production is a manufacturing philosophy and inventory management system in which materials, components, and sub-assemblies are delivered and produced only when they are needed in the production process — not before. JIT aims to eliminate waste in all its forms (overproduction, waiting, unnecessary transport, excess inventory, over-processing, defects, and unused talent — the 'seven wastes' of the Toyota Production System). JIT originated at Toyota in Japan in the post-war period and was a central component of the Toyota Production System (TPS) that underpins lean manufacturing.

The defining characteristic of JIT is that inventory is minimised throughout the production system. In a JIT system, materials arrive from suppliers at the production facility immediately before they are needed; work-in-progress inventory between production stages is minimal; and finished goods inventory is small because products are produced to order (pull production) rather than in anticipation of future demand (push production). A kanban system — a visual signalling mechanism (originally cards, now often electronic signals) — controls the flow of materials: a workstation signals its upstream supplier (internal or external) to deliver more material only when it has consumed its current stock. This pull system ensures material only flows when it is actually needed.

JIT production is contrasted with just-in-case (JIC) inventory management, in which large buffer stocks of materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods are held to protect production against supply disruptions, demand variability, and quality failures. JIC is the traditional Western manufacturing approach: carry enough inventory that production is never stopped by a shortage. JIC hides problems — buffer stocks mask quality issues and process inefficiencies; JIT forces them to the surface, because there is no buffer to absorb disruption.

JIT depends critically on reliable supplier relationships. Since there is no buffer stock to absorb late or defective deliveries, suppliers must deliver exactly the right quantity, of perfect quality, exactly when required. This requires: long-term, trust-based relationships with a small number of key suppliers (rather than transactional price-driven relationships with many competing suppliers); suppliers located close to the production facility to minimise delivery time and transport risk; joint quality assurance processes (suppliers deliver quality at source, eliminating the need for incoming inspection); and collaborative planning and information sharing (suppliers must know the production schedule well in advance).

Advantages of JIT: reduces inventory holding costs (capital tied up in stock, storage space, insurance, obsolescence risk); reduces floor space requirements; forces resolution of quality and process problems (no buffer to hide them); improves cash flow; and reduces the risk of producing obsolete products. Risks: vulnerability to supply chain disruptions — a supplier quality failure, natural disaster, or transport disruption stops production immediately (as dramatically demonstrated during COVID-19 and the 2021 global semiconductor shortage); requires highly reliable, geographically proximate suppliers; and requires very stable production schedules.

quiz.question: Define just-in-time production and explain how a kanban system supports it.
quiz.markscheme: JIT is a production philosophy in which materials and components are delivered and produced only when needed — minimising inventory at all stages. A kanban is a visual signal (card, bin, or electronic signal) that authorises the movement or production of a defined quantity of material. When a workstation consumes its buffer stock to a trigger level, the kanban signals the upstream workstation or supplier to replenish it — exactly the right quantity, at the right time. This creates a pull system: material flows downstream only when consumption triggers the signal. Without kanban (a push system), material is produced according to a schedule, regardless of actual downstream consumption, risking overproduction and excess inventory.

quiz.question: Distinguish between JIT and JIC inventory management approaches, explaining the philosophy behind each.
quiz.markscheme: JIT: inventory is minimised; materials arrive exactly when needed; driven by pull (demand triggers production). Philosophy: eliminate all waste, including inventory as a form of waste that conceals problems. Forces quality and process problems to be resolved rather than buffered. JIC: large buffer stocks held at all stages; designed to protect production against supply variability, quality failures, and demand fluctuation. Philosophy: reliability of supply and production is the priority; inventory cost is justified by the insurance it provides. JIT is more efficient but more vulnerable; JIC is less efficient but more resilient. COVID-19 supply chain disruptions in 2020-21 exposed the vulnerability of highly JIT-dependent systems.

quiz.question: Explain why JIT production requires fundamentally different supplier relationships than traditional purchasing.
quiz.markscheme: JIT eliminates buffer stock, so supplier reliability is critical — a single late or defective delivery stops production. This requires: long-term partnership relationships (not transactional) built on trust, shared information, and mutual investment; suppliers located geographically close to the production facility to enable frequent small deliveries; quality at source (suppliers provide defect-free material — no incoming inspection buffer); joint production planning and information sharing so suppliers can plan ahead; and collaborative problem-solving when issues arise. Traditional price-driven, adversarial, multi-supplier purchasing is incompatible with JIT — the cost savings from competitive tendering are far outweighed by the costs of unreliable supply in a JIT system.

quiz.question: Analyse the supply chain risks of JIT production and describe how the COVID-19 pandemic exposed these vulnerabilities.
quiz.markscheme: JIT's vulnerability: no buffer stock means any supply disruption (supplier quality failure, transport disruption, natural disaster, geopolitical event) immediately halts production. COVID-19 exposed this: factory closures in supply regions (particularly China and Southeast Asia) immediately disrupted JIT supply chains globally; shipping container shortages and port congestion made just-in-time delivery impossible; demand shifts (e.g. massive consumer electronics demand) overwhelmed suppliers without buffer inventory. Semiconductor shortages caused automotive plants globally to halt production for months — Toyota and others could not build cars waiting for chips. Response: many companies are now re-evaluating pure JIT, building strategic buffer stocks for critical components, diversifying geographic supply, and investing in supply chain visibility. The trade-off between JIT efficiency and JIC resilience is now recognised as a strategic risk management decision, not just an operations choice.

quiz.question: Discuss the conditions under which JIT production is most and least appropriate.
quiz.markscheme: Most appropriate when: production volumes are high and stable (predictable pull signal); suppliers are geographically close and highly reliable; product designs are stable (frequent design changes would create obsolescence risk in any inventory held); the product is time-sensitive (perishable food, fashion) making inventory especially undesirable; and the organisation has the process discipline and supplier relationships to maintain zero-defect supply. Least appropriate when: supply chains are long and geographically dispersed; components are sourced from geopolitically unstable regions; demand is highly variable or seasonal; suppliers are unreliable; or the product has a long and complex supply chain with multiple tiers of suppliers (the semiconductor supply chain, for example, has lead times of 6-18 months, incompatible with strict JIT).

title: Lean production
objectives: Define lean production and identify its origins in the Toyota Production System.
Identify the seven wastes (muda) of lean production and give examples of each.
Describe key lean tools including value stream mapping, 5S, and kaizen.
Analyse how lean principles can be applied to product design as well as production.
Evaluate the challenges of implementing lean production in an organisation.
summary:
Lean production is a manufacturing philosophy and systematic method for eliminating waste within a manufacturing system while simultaneously delivering value to the customer. It derives from the Toyota Production System (TPS), developed primarily by Taiichi Ohno and Eiji Toyoda at Toyota from the 1940s onwards and codified for a Western audience by Womack, Jones, and Roos in 'The Machine That Changed the World' (1990). Lean production has since been applied beyond manufacturing to service industries, healthcare, software development, and government.

The fundamental concept of lean is to identify and eliminate muda (waste) — any activity that consumes resources without creating value for the customer. Taiichi Ohno defined seven types of waste: Overproduction (producing more than is needed — considered the worst waste as it generates all others); Waiting (idle time when people or machines are waiting for material, information, or equipment); Unnecessary Transport (moving material more than necessary); Over-processing (doing more work than the customer requires); Excess Inventory (holding more stock than the minimum required — hides problems); Unnecessary Motion (unnecessary movement by people at work); and Defects (producing defective products that must be scrapped or reworked). A widely used eighth waste is Unused talent (failing to use employees' knowledge and skills).

Lean tools include: Value Stream Mapping (VSM) — mapping all the steps (value-adding and non-value-adding) required to bring a product from raw material to customer, visualising waste and identifying improvement opportunities; 5S (Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardise, Sustain) — a workplace organisation methodology that eliminates clutter, establishes standard locations for all items, and maintains a clean, ordered working environment as the foundation for reliable, high-quality production; Kaizen (continuous improvement) — a philosophy and set of practices for ongoing incremental improvement, involving all employees in identifying and implementing small improvements in their own work; Single-Minute Exchange of Die (SMED) — reducing production changeover time to enable flexible, small-batch production without setup waste; Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) — proactive maintenance to maximise equipment availability and reliability.

Lean principles apply not only to production processes but also to product design — 'lean design' or 'design for manufacturability' (DfM) means designing products in ways that are intrinsically less wasteful to produce. Design choices that reduce part count, simplify assembly sequences, use standard components, and avoid over-specified tolerances directly reduce production waste. The most powerful waste reduction happens when design and engineering collaborate to eliminate waste before production begins — once a design is released to production, many waste opportunities are locked in.

Implementing lean in an organisation faces cultural and organisational challenges. Lean requires a fundamental cultural change: from a culture of firefighting (reacting to problems as they occur) to one of prevention, continuous improvement, and employee engagement. Middle managers may resist changes that reduce their control. Workers accustomed to buffer stocks may feel exposed by their removal. Improvement suggestions from the shop floor must be genuinely welcomed and acted upon. Sustained leadership commitment is essential — lean cannot be implemented as a one-off project; it requires continuous organisational attention and discipline to maintain.

quiz.question: Identify and describe three of Ohno's seven wastes of lean production, giving a manufacturing example of each.
quiz.markscheme: Accept any three: Overproduction (producing more units than customer orders — e.g. a factory producing components to a weekly schedule regardless of actual orders, creating finished goods that sit in store); Waiting (a worker waiting for a machine to finish, or a machine waiting for material — e.g. an assembly operator idle because the previous station is slow); Defects (producing defective products requiring rework or scrap — e.g. a welding defect discovered at final inspection requiring rework of all affected units); Excess inventory (holding more material, WIP, or finished goods than needed — e.g. a large stack of sheet metal at a press station far exceeding a day's requirements); Unnecessary motion (an operator walking long distances to retrieve tools — e.g. tools stored across the factory floor from the workstation where they are used). Each waste should be described and linked to a specific example.

quiz.question: Describe the 5S methodology and explain how it contributes to lean production.
quiz.markscheme: 5S: Sort (separate needed from unneeded items; remove everything unnecessary from the workplace); Set in order (assign a specific place to every item that is needed — 'a place for everything and everything in its place'); Shine (clean the workplace — cleaning as inspection, revealing equipment problems early); Standardise (make the first three S's a routine through standard procedures and visual management); Sustain (maintain discipline to uphold the standards, through audits and leadership). Contribution to lean: 5S eliminates time wasted searching for tools or materials; reduces defects caused by a dirty or disorganised workplace; makes abnormal conditions visible immediately (deviations from the standard location are instantly visible); and creates the foundation of reliability and discipline on which more advanced lean tools depend.

quiz.question: Explain value stream mapping (VSM) and describe how it helps identify waste in a production process.
quiz.markscheme: VSM is a visual mapping tool that traces the flow of material and information required to bring a product from raw material to the customer. The map shows every step — value-adding (transforming the product towards the final specification) and non-value-adding (storage, waiting, inspection, transport). For each step, key data is recorded: cycle time, changeover time, reliability, inventory levels. The 'current state' map reveals where waste is concentrated — large inventory buffers between steps, long waiting times, defect rates requiring rework. The 'future state' map redesigns the flow to eliminate identified wastes — typically involving JIT flow between steps, elimination of unnecessary moves, and reduction of batch sizes. VSM provides a shared visual understanding of where improvement effort will have the greatest impact.

quiz.question: Analyse how lean design (design for manufacturability) can reduce production waste.
quiz.markscheme: Design for manufacturability reduces waste before production begins — when waste is most cheaply eliminated. Lean design strategies: reducing part count (fewer parts = fewer assembly operations = less material waste and assembly time); using standard components and materials (reduces procurement complexity and obsolescence waste); designing assembly sequences that minimise difficult or error-prone operations (reducing defect rates); specifying appropriate tolerances (avoiding over-tight tolerances that require expensive precision processes where standard tolerance is sufficient); designing for automated assembly (reducing variability and labour waste); and designing for right-first-time quality (poka-yoke features that make it impossible to assemble parts incorrectly). The leverage of lean design is much higher than lean production improvement — it is cheaper to change a dimension on a drawing than to rearrange a production line.

quiz.question: Discuss two cultural challenges organisations face when implementing lean production.
quiz.markscheme: Accept any two with discussion: Firefighting culture vs. prevention culture: many organisations are accustomed to reacting to problems as they arise; lean requires investing time in prevention (root cause analysis, poka-yoke, preventive maintenance) rather than heroic firefighting. Cultural change is difficult — firefighters are often celebrated as heroes while prevention work is invisible and unrewarded. Middle management resistance: lean often challenges traditional management power structures — it delegates improvement authority to the shop floor, reducing the role of middle managers as information controllers. Those whose authority is threatened may resist actively or passively. Sustaining improvement: initial enthusiasm for lean workshops can produce rapid short-term gains, but sustaining the discipline requires ongoing leadership attention, recognition systems, and embedding lean thinking in daily routines — many lean implementations regress after initial improvement because this sustained commitment is not maintained.

title: Computer integrated manufacturing (CIM)
objectives: Define computer integrated manufacturing (CIM) and describe its components.
Explain how CAD/CAM integration enables CIM.
Describe the role of robots and automated guided vehicles (AGVs) in CIM.
Analyse the advantages and limitations of CIM for manufacturing competitiveness.
Discuss the implications of CIM for the design of products and manufacturing facilities.
summary:
Computer integrated manufacturing (CIM) is the comprehensive integration of computer technologies throughout all aspects of the manufacturing enterprise — from product design and engineering through production planning, shop floor control, and quality management, to delivery logistics. In a fully integrated CIM environment, digital data flows seamlessly between all these functions: a design change in CAD propagates automatically to manufacturing programmes, production schedules, material orders, and inspection plans. CIM represents the realisation of the 'digital thread' that connects every stage of the product life cycle.

The core of CIM is the integration of CAD (Computer-Aided Design) and CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing). In a CAD/CAM integrated system, the 3D model created by the designer is the single authoritative source of product geometry. CAM software reads the CAD geometry and generates machine toolpaths directly, without the need for separate programming or manual dimension transfer. This eliminates a major source of error and enables near-instantaneous response to design changes — update the CAD model, regenerate the CAM programme, and the machines can begin cutting the new version within hours. Extended CAD/CAM integration includes: Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP, which automatically generates manufacturing process plans from product geometry); computer-aided quality inspection (CMM — coordinate measuring machines — that check finished parts against the CAD model); and product lifecycle management (PLM) software that manages all product data across the enterprise.

Robots are a central element of CIM. In a CIM environment, robots perform repetitive, precise operations (welding, painting, assembly, material handling) under computer control, receiving instructions from the manufacturing execution system (MES) that coordinates all shop floor activities. Automated guided vehicles (AGVs) — wheeled robots that navigate factory floors autonomously — handle material transport between workstations and stores, eliminating manual material handling and the associated waste of waiting and unnecessary motion. Modern CIM systems use a combination of fixed robots, collaborative robots (cobots), and AGVs, integrated through a factory-wide information system.

The advantages of CIM include: high and consistent product quality (automation eliminates human variability); rapid response to design changes (digital integration propagates changes quickly); ability to operate 24 hours a day without fatigue; reduced labour cost per unit in high-volume production; improved data for production monitoring and continuous improvement; and the ability to handle product variants efficiently in a flexible manufacturing system. Limitations include: very high capital investment in hardware, software, integration, and commissioning; complexity of integration between different systems (often from different vendors); requirement for highly skilled maintenance, programming, and engineering staff; vulnerability to system failures (a software or hardware failure can stop the whole integrated system); and inflexibility to accommodate product designs or processes that were not anticipated during system design.

CIM has implications for product design: to exploit CIM's capabilities fully, products should be designed for automated manufacture. This means: design for automated assembly (DFAA) — features that facilitate robot pickup, orientation, and placement; minimising asymmetric parts (which are harder for robots to orient consistently); designing for right-first-time quality; and ensuring that CAD geometry is clean and manufacturing-ready. The integration between product design and manufacturing engineering that CIM enables means that design and manufacturing teams must work collaboratively from the earliest stages of product development — the traditional 'over the wall' model (design throws the design to manufacturing) is incompatible with realising CIM's full potential.

quiz.question: Define CIM and explain how the integration of CAD and CAM supports it.
quiz.markscheme: CIM is the comprehensive integration of computer technologies throughout all aspects of the manufacturing enterprise — design, engineering, production planning, shop floor control, and quality management. CAD/CAM integration is core to CIM: the 3D CAD model is the single authoritative source of product geometry; CAM software reads this geometry directly to generate machine toolpaths, eliminating manual dimension transfer and associated errors; design changes in CAD propagate automatically to updated CAM programmes. This integration enables rapid response to design changes, eliminates transcription errors, and creates a 'digital thread' connecting design to production.

quiz.question: Describe the role of automated guided vehicles (AGVs) in a CIM manufacturing environment.
quiz.markscheme: AGVs are wheeled robots that navigate factory floors autonomously, transporting materials between workstations, stores, and loading areas without human operators. In CIM, AGVs receive routing instructions from the manufacturing execution system (MES) based on current production requirements — they deliver exactly the material needed, to exactly the workstation needed, exactly when it is needed. This eliminates manual material handling (waste of unnecessary motion and transport), supports JIT material flow, and reduces waiting waste. AGVs can operate continuously, adapt routes in real time based on production changes, and are tracked by the MES providing full material traceability.

quiz.question: Analyse three advantages of CIM for a manufacturer of consumer electronics compared to a less automated facility.
quiz.markscheme: Quality consistency: automated operations do not vary with fatigue, time of day, or operator skill — the same weld, soldering operation, or assembly step is performed identically every cycle, producing more consistent quality; 24-hour operation: CIM enables continuous production without labour constraints, increasing throughput and reducing unit cost; Rapid design change response: CAD/CAM integration means a design change can be reflected in production within hours rather than weeks (redrawing toolpaths, updating assembly programmes automatically); Data for continuous improvement: automated systems generate detailed production data (cycle times, defect rates, machine utilisation) enabling systematic identification and elimination of waste. Each advantage should be explained in the electronics manufacturing context.

quiz.question: Discuss the design implications of CIM for products intended to be manufactured in a highly automated facility.
quiz.markscheme: Products designed for CIM/automated manufacture should: minimise part count (fewer parts = fewer automated handling operations); use Design for Automated Assembly (DFAA) principles — symmetric parts (robot cannot misalign them), self-locating features (parts that align themselves when placed), and features enabling robotic pick-and-place; avoid materials or fasteners that require manual handling (e.g. flexible parts like cables and foam are difficult for robots); design for right-first-time quality (poka-yoke features that prevent incorrect assembly); ensure clean, manufacturing-ready CAD geometry for automatic CAM programming. The close collaboration of design engineering and manufacturing engineering from the earliest design stage is essential to realise CIM benefits — product designers must understand automated process capabilities and constraints as part of their design practice.

quiz.question: Evaluate the limitations of CIM as a manufacturing strategy.
quiz.markscheme: Capital cost: CIM systems require very large investment in hardware (robots, AGVs, CMMs), software (CAD/CAM/MES/PLM), integration, and commissioning — only justified at high production volumes. Complexity: integrating systems from different vendors is technically complex; maintenance requires multi-disciplinary expertise; software integration issues can cause system-wide failures. Inflexibility: CIM systems are optimised for the product range anticipated at design — accommodating a substantially different new product may require expensive re-engineering of the system. Vulnerability: system failures (hardware, software, or network) can halt the entire integrated facility; compared to a less automated facility where human workers can adapt to problems, CIM is more brittle. Skills requirement: programming, commissioning, and maintaining CIM systems requires highly skilled engineers — not universally available and expensive. A balanced evaluation considers both advantages and these specific limitations.

title: Quality management
objectives: Define quality in the context of product design and manufacture.
Distinguish between quality control (QC) and quality assurance (QA).
Explain the principles of Total Quality Management (TQM).
Describe statistical process control (SPC) and explain how control charts are used.
Analyse how quality management systems such as ISO 9001 support commercial and design practice.
summary:
Quality is a multidimensional concept in product design and manufacture. It refers to the degree to which a product or service meets the needs and expectations of customers — encompassing fitness for purpose (the product does what it is designed to do), reliability (it continues to do so over its design life), conformance (it meets defined specifications), durability (it resists wear and failure), aesthetics (it looks and feels right), and perceived quality (the impression of quality formed by customers). Quality is not simply a matter of the highest possible specification — a budget product can be high quality if it reliably meets the expectations and needs of its intended users at its price point.

Quality Control (QC) and Quality Assurance (QA) are distinct but complementary approaches. QC is reactive and product-focused: it involves inspecting or testing finished products (or in-process products) to detect and remove defective items before they reach the customer. QC answers the question: 'did we make it right?' QA is proactive and process-focused: it involves implementing systems and processes that prevent defects from being produced in the first place. QA answers the question: 'are we set up to make it right consistently?' Modern quality thinking emphasises QA over QC: preventing defects is always cheaper and more effective than detecting and correcting them.

Total Quality Management (TQM) is a comprehensive management philosophy and system in which quality is the responsibility of every person in the organisation, not just the quality department. TQM principles, developed from the work of W. Edwards Deming, Joseph Juran, and others, include: customer focus (quality is defined by customer needs and expectations); continuous improvement (quality is always being improved, never considered complete — the 'Kaizen' mindset); involvement of all employees (everyone is responsible for quality); process approach (understanding and managing processes, not just outcomes); evidence-based decision making (using data to understand and improve processes); and leadership commitment (senior management must champion and model quality values). TQM transformed manufacturing quality in Japan (which adopted Deming's principles in the 1950s) and eventually globally.

Statistical Process Control (SPC) is a quality control methodology that uses statistical methods to monitor and control manufacturing processes in real time. Rather than inspecting 100% of output (costly) or inspecting a fixed sample after the fact (reacting after defects are produced), SPC continuously monitors key process variables. Control charts — the central tool of SPC — plot measured values of a process variable over time alongside upper and lower control limits (calculated from process variation data). As long as measurements fall within control limits and show no non-random patterns, the process is considered 'in control' — producing consistent quality. When a measurement falls outside control limits or a non-random pattern appears (e.g. a run of points trending upwards), the process is flagged as out of control and operators investigate and correct the cause before defects are produced. SPC turns inspection from a reactive to a preventive activity.

ISO 9001 is the international standard for quality management systems (QMS). It provides a framework of documented processes, procedures, and responsibilities that organisations implement to consistently provide products and services that meet customer and regulatory requirements. ISO 9001 certification is awarded by accredited third-party certification bodies after auditing the organisation's QMS implementation. Certification is required by many industrial customers as a condition of supply — particularly in automotive, aerospace, medical devices, and defence. ISO 9001 does not specify what quality level is required, only that the organisation has a documented, consistently followed system for managing quality. Its adoption forces organisations to document processes, train staff, monitor performance, and systematically address non-conformances.

quiz.question: Distinguish between quality control and quality assurance in manufacturing, explaining which approach is generally preferred and why.
quiz.markscheme: QC (Quality Control): reactive and product-focused — inspecting or testing finished/in-process products to detect and remove defects before they reach the customer. Answers 'did we make it right?' QA (Quality Assurance): proactive and process-focused — implementing systems, procedures, and controls that prevent defects from occurring. Answers 'are we set up to make it right consistently?' QA is generally preferred: preventing defects is always cheaper than detecting and correcting them; scrap and rework waste materials, time, and capacity; defects that escape inspection reach customers and cause damage to brand and relationships. QC remains necessary as a safety net, but the goal of quality improvement is to make QC progressively less necessary by improving processes.

quiz.question: Explain three principles of Total Quality Management (TQM) and describe how each would affect practice in a manufacturing organisation.
quiz.markscheme: Accept any three: Customer focus (quality is defined by customer needs — the organisation regularly researches customer requirements and measures customer satisfaction; design decisions are evaluated against customer criteria); Continuous improvement/Kaizen (improvement is never complete — every employee participates in identifying and implementing small improvements in their own work area; kaizen events and suggestion systems are institutionalised); Involvement of all employees (quality is not the quality department's responsibility alone — every worker is responsible for the quality of their own output; quality metrics are visible to all and improvement is a shared goal); Evidence-based decision making (process problems are analysed using data — control charts, root cause analysis, Pareto analysis — not guesswork). Each principle should be explained with a concrete operational example.

quiz.question: Describe how a control chart is used in statistical process control and explain what action is taken when a measurement falls outside the control limits.
quiz.markscheme: A control chart plots measurements of a process variable (e.g. the diameter of a machined component) over time. Upper Control Limit (UCL) and Lower Control Limit (LCL) are calculated from historical process variation data (typically ±3 standard deviations from the mean). As long as measurements fall randomly within these limits, the process is 'in control' and no action is needed. When a measurement falls outside the control limits, or when a non-random pattern appears (a run of points trending in one direction, or consistently above/below the centre line), this signals an assignable cause of variation — something has changed in the process. The operator stops the process, investigates and identifies the root cause, corrects it, and verifies the process has returned to control before resuming production.

quiz.question: Analyse how ISO 9001 certification can benefit a manufacturing company seeking to supply to automotive OEMs.
quiz.markscheme: Automotive OEMs (original equipment manufacturers) require ISO 9001 (and typically its automotive extension IATF 16949) as a minimum condition of approval as a supplier. Benefits: access to customers who require certification (without it, the company cannot supply to this sector — certification opens the market); demonstrates systematic quality management to customers, reducing their supplier qualification costs; internal benefits of the QMS framework (documented processes, training records, non-conformance management, corrective action systems — all improve operational discipline); audit readiness (the certification audit and annual surveillance audits drive continuous improvement and discipline in maintaining the QMS). Limitation: certification demonstrates the existence of a QMS, not necessarily the level of product quality — a well-documented mediocre quality system can achieve certification. Additional automotive standards (IATF 16949, APQP, PPAP processes) provide a more demanding quality framework for the sector.

quiz.question: Discuss how W. Edwards Deming's contributions changed the practice of quality management globally.
quiz.markscheme: Deming, a statistician, developed statistical process control methods while working with Walter Shewhart at Bell Labs and applied them to manufacturing. After World War II, his ideas were rejected by US industry but enthusiastically adopted in Japan, where JUSE (Japanese Union of Scientists and Engineers) invited him to teach. Japanese manufacturers applied Deming's statistical methods and management philosophy — customer focus, process thinking, continuous improvement, reducing variation — achieving dramatic quality and productivity improvements that transformed Japanese industry's global competitiveness by the 1970s and 1980s. The success of Japanese quality prompted Western manufacturers to relearn and adopt TQM principles in the 1980s. Deming's 14 Points for Management provide a comprehensive philosophy of quality-driven management that goes beyond statistical tools to address organisational culture, leadership, and employee motivation. His legacy is the global quality movement and the integration of statistical methods into everyday manufacturing practice.
